text: AKCHAR BUNAR, a town of Karamania, 4 m. W of Tris Maden, at the head of a small stream flowing NW to the Soghla Gol.
AKCHEH CALEH, a small fishing-v. on the S coast of the Black sea, in the pashalik of Trebisond, 12 m. NW of Trebisond.
AKCHELAR, a small town of Asiatic Turkey, in Karamania, 13 m. SE by S of Beishehr, on the Beishehr Su.

AKCHIKEVI, a v. on the plain of Troy, near the r. bank of the Kunar Su, the ancient Andreios, a little above its junction with the Mendere Chai, the ancient Simois. AK DAGH, [i.e. 'White mount,'] a mountain of Persia, in the prov. of Ghilan, belonging to the Elburz range. Lead is wrought in it.—There is a mountain of the same name in the central chain of the Taurus range.-And the name repeatedly occurs, under the forms Ak Dagh, Ak Tagh, or Ak Togh, in Oriental geography.
AK DERBEND, a pass from the Atak to Upper Khorásán, at the point where the Elburz range is pierced by the Mash-hed river. The name signifies in Turkish 'White defile.'

AK DRIN, a river of Albania, which rises among the mountains which cover the country between Mount Troizza and Mount Hajla, on the northern boundaries of the pashalik of Scutari; passes near Ipek in a SE by S course, and flows through a fertile plain several miles in breadth, till it receives the Topolavha; after which it turns to the SW, flows through the Val Salki, and unites with the Kara Drin or 'Black Drin' near the v. of Brut.
AKEEKSEEBA, a river in the NW territory of America, flowing into the NW side of Spirit lake, in N lat. 46° 45'.
AKEENY, a river of Western Africa, supposed to be a branch of the Lagos, which Lander crossed about 2 m. from Afura.
AKELEY, a p. and township in Bucks, 2½ m. NE of Buckingham. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 362.
AKEN. See ACKEN.
AKENHAM, a p. in Suffolk, 3½ m. N by W of Ipswich. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 117.

AKEREY, a town of Kurdistan, of about 500 houses, surrounded by gardens. It is built at the foot of a rocky mountain-one of the range called El Khair, or the mountains of Amadiyah—on the summit of which is a strong castle. AKERMAN, ACKERMAN, OF AKKERMANNE, a fortified town in the Russian prov. of Bessarabia, near the coast of the Black sea and the mouth of the Dniester; in N lat. 46° 11′, E long. 30° 23′. It stands on a point of land projecting from the W side of what is called the liman or gulf of the Dniester, or Lake Ovidova, 8 m. from its entrance; and is commanded by a castle crowning an eminence in the neighbourhood. The town is ill-built, but contains a number of mosques, and several Armenian and Greek churches. It is a place of some trade, exporting salt, corn, wool, hides, butter, wine, and wood; but the port is inaccessible by vessels drawing more than 7 ft. water, and the difficulty of navigating the river restricts its commerce. The liman yields 7,000,000 pouds of salt annually. In 1830, 18 vessels in ballast, and 90 with cargoes, arrived at A. The total value of the cargoes imported that year was 54,868 roubles. In 1832, the imports amounted to 42,967 roubles; in 1833, to 180,653 roubles; or about one-tenth of the whole imports into the Black sea ports of Russia. The pop. is variously estimated by different travellers at from 10,000 to 20,000.—A. is supposed by Marigny to be built on the site of the ancient Ophioussa. It has borne successively the classical names of Alba Julia, Kastrum Album, and Hermonoclis. By the Poles it is called Beilgorod or 'White city;' and by the Tatars, Akkermane or 'White castle.' It has acquired recent historical celebrity by the treaty concluded here, Oc- AKH tober 6, 1826, betwixt Russia and Turkey, by which the Porte ceded to Russia some of its principal fortresses in Asia; acknowledged the political organization which Russia approved of for Servia, Moldavia, and Wallachia; and opened the Dardanelles to the Russian navy.
AKERSLOOT, a v. of Holland, in the prov. of North Holland, 6 m. S by W of Alkmaar. Pop. 854.
AKETA, a large town of Western Africa, on the r. bank of the Chad, a day's sail above its confluence with the Quorra. The river opposite this town is 1,470 yards wide.—Allen.

AK GEDUK, or LEWAN CHAI, a pass or defile in the mountains of Tárom in Persia, descending from the high table-land of Media into the lower country bordering on the Caspian. It is nearly 12 m. in length, having a very winding course in the general direction of N 60° E.—Rawlinson. AK GOL, [i.e. 'White lake,'] a lake between Eregli on the S coast of the Black sea, and the Kara Tagh to the E. Mr. Hamilton found it "a large swampy lake" in the month of August.
AKHAHAR, a town of Arabia Petræa, on the route from Mecca to Jerusalem, in N lat. 27° 27', E long. 35° 33'.

AKHALKALAKI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash, and 28 m. SE of Akhalzike, on a small affluent of the Kur. It is said to have been once the finest city in Armenia; but is now a small place, with a pop. not exceeding 1,000.—There is another town of the same name in Russian Georgia, on the road from Teflis to Gori.

AKHALZIKE, or AKHALCHICK, a district of Georgian Armenia, lying to the N of Erzerum, in an enclosed valley of the Keldir mountains, which contains the sources and the upper course of the Kur river. Hence in old maps this district appears under the name of Keldir or Chaldir; but in modern times, it has received its present appellation from the city or town of Akhalzike, or Akhishkah, [i. e. 'the New fortress,'] the residence of the Turkish beglerbeg or pasha, now included within the Russian boundary. Of the topography of this mountainous district very little is known. The pashalik of Keldir was at one time, after that of Baghdad, the largest in Asiatic Turkey; extending to the Russian frontier on the N; to the Persian on the E; to the S over all the Kurdish beys; and to the SW as far as the vicinity of Arabkir. The modern Turkish pashalik of A. has been recently united with that of Kars; but the district of A. is bounded by the Georgian districts of Mingrelia and Imeretia on the N, the entire basin of the Rioni or Phasis lying within the Russian line; by Georgia on the E; by the pashalik of Kars on the S; by that of Trebisond on the W; and by the district of Gouriel, which separates it from the SE corner of the Black sea, on the NW. It is a mountainous district, in which the highest crests attain an elevation of nearly 8,000 ft. above sea-level. The winter is long and severe; while the heat of summer is very great. Maize, wheat, barley, flax, tobacco, and cotton are cultivated; fruits are abundant, and the grape in particular attains great size and excellence. The exports consist of cattle, skins, tallow, honey, and wax. The major part of the pop. are of Georgian descent, and profess the Christian religion. The principal places still remaining in the possession of the Turks are the fortress of Ardanuji in the valley of Narimani; the city of Otissi in the same valley; the town of Artani on the road from Akhalzike to Batum and Gonieh, on the coast; and the fort of Khertwissi on the r. bank of the Kur. The districts recently ceded to Russia by the treaty of Adrianople are on the SW frontier of Russian Georgia, and comprise an area of 7,000 square versts, with the fortresses of Azghur and Akhalkalaki.—The latter town rises on the banks of the Dlaki, a little stream flowing into the Kur, 115 m. SW of Teflis. It has a pop. of 13,500, of whom about 60 families are Jews. It is remarkable for its fine mosque, built by Sultan Ahmed in imitation of that of St. Sophia in Constantinople.

AKHARSU, a river of Asiatic Turkey, which rises in various head-springs to the N and E of Afiúm Karahisar, and flows SSE in a meandering course to lake Eberdi or the Eber Gol, lying between the Emir Dagh on the NE, and the Sultan Dagh on the SW. It probably connects that lake with the Ak Shehr Gol, which lies in the same valley further to the SE, and loses itself in the continuous marsh of which these lakes appear to form part. A mile below Afium Karahisar, Kinneir found the A. "a sluggish river, deep, but not above 10 yards in breadth." AKHDAR (JEBEL), [i.e. 'Green mountain,'] an extensive and lofty chain of the Arabian peninsula, in Oman, in the prov. of Nizzuwah or Neswah, stretching E and W, under the parallel of 23° N, a distance of 30 m., with a maximum breadth of 14 m. A great proportion of the surface of the range is bare limestone rock; but the valleys are well-cultivated and produce abundance of fruit, especially white and black grapes, almonds, and figs, besides grain. Wellsted estimated the elevation of Shirazi, a town at the eastern extremity of this range, at 6,187 ft., while it is from 800 to 1,000 ft. below the level of the summit of the greater part of the chain. The Beni Riyam Arabs inhabit this range. - Wellsted.

AKHISSA'R, or AKHISA'R, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Sarukhan, in N lat. 39° 5', E long. 27° 48'; 69 m. NE of Smyrna, and 45 m. SE of Pergamo. It stands on a piece of rising ground slightly elevated above the surrounding alluvial and marshy plain, near the Hermus, a small river which forms a tributary of the Caicus, in the centre of a plain 20 m. in breadth, surrounded with mountains; and is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Thyatira, and of the more ancient Pelopia or Euhippa. When visited by Captain Keppel, in 1830, it consisted of a few narrow, dirty streets, containing about 1,000 houses, constructed of turf or sunburnt brick, and very low and rudely built, and 200 or 300 huts. The only buildings of importance were the mutsellim's palace, several marble mosques, a Greek and an Armenian church, two Greek schools, two public baths, and a ruined castle. With the exception of a few inscriptions on marble, and some prostrate columns, it retains no relics of its former splendour. But it is rapidly recovering somewhat of its former prosperity; and Captain Newbold, its latest visitor, in a memoir recently read before the Asiatic society, estimates the pop. at 10,000, of whom 2,000 are Greeks. The prevailing language is the Turkish. The environs are extremely fertile, producing grain and immense crops of the finest cotton, the manufacture of which constitutes one of the most important industrial occupations of the inhabitants. Grapes and tamarinds are also extensively cultivated. The vicinity of the town is adorned with many fine ruins. On an eminence are the remains of the Turkish castle from which the town derives its name; signifying 'White castle.' [Spon.]—There is another Akhisar in the sanjak of Bosnaserai, on the Sakaria, 24 m. S by E of Nicomedia.—Akchah Shehr, on the S coast of the Black sea, is designated in some maps by this name.—And Croya on the banks of the Ismo, in the Albanian sanjak of Ochrida, sometimes bears this name.

AKHLAT, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Van, situated at the foot of the Seïban-Dagh, on the NW shore of the lake of Van; 20 m. N by E of Bitlis, and 15 m. W of Ad-il-jivaz. It is of great an- I. AKI tiquity, and was, in the days of its splendour, the residence of the kings of Armenia. It now consists of about 1,000 houses, surrounded by a double wall and ditch. The climate is cold, but its environs are adorned with vineyards and gardens, and produce nuts and apples in great perfection. It is governed by a bey.—Its early history presents it as the scene of long-continued conflict between the Greeks, Persians, and Armenians. In 1228 it was taken and devastated by Djelal-Eddin, and in 1246 an earthquake completed its ruin. In 1548 it fell into the hands of Aladdin, and was retained by his descendants for a hundred years. It subsequently came under the dominion of the Turks. The old town of A. is at a little distance from the modern town, higher up the hills.
AKHMAR (DEIR EL), a Maronite v. in the pashalik of Tripoli, on the lowest slope of Lebanon, 3 hours NW from Baalbec, and 5 hours from the summit of
AKHMET, a town of Russian Georgia, in the dis- Lebanon. trict of Kakheti, 15 m. NW of Telaf.

AKHMIN, or ECHMIN, a town of Middle Egypt, near the r. bank of the Nile, in N lat. 26° 38'. It has about 3,000 inhabitants, of whom 2,000 are Catholic Copts. It is on the site of the Chemnis of Herodotus, the Panoplis of later writers. When Pococke visited A. it was the residence of an independent emir. It is a town of some trade, and has a manufactory of coarse cotton cloth.

AKHO, or Ako, a valley in the pashalik of Kars, through which a route from Batum to Kars descends from the Kolowah Dagh into the Kulah or Agirah valley. The Georgian language is spoken in this valley. Rye and Indian corn are cultivated, and a small quantity of silk is produced.
AKHTAMAR. See AGHTAMAR.
AKHTYRKA. See ACHTYRKA.
AKHUM, a town of Bokhara on the l. bank of the Jihun, 82 m. NW of Balkh.

AKI, a district of Nifon in Japan, on the N coast of the Swumada sea or channel. Kæmpfer says it is a mountainous and barren tract, 21 days' journey in length from S to N; that corn, rice, and gokokf will hardly grow in it, but that it abounds with woods

AKILI, or AKKILLE, a large v. of Arabistan, in a and forests. fine valley of the same name, through which the Karun flows, in a broad and tranquil stream, on first emerging from the mountains, and before its bending S above Shushter. The valley is one vast corn-field, studded with villages and date-groves. AKILUNDA, ACHELUNDA, or AQUELUNDA, a lake of reputed existence in Congo, at the foot of the Serras de Prata or Silver mountains, nearly under the parallel of 7° 30' S. It is said to be the source of the Barbela, one of the tributaries of the Zaire; and to give origin likewise to the Kwansa and the

AKIM, a small state of Western Africa, between Lelunda rivers. Ashantee or Amina on the NW; Aguapim on the E; and the Fantee territories on the S. Bosman says it was represented to him as a very large country, and as remarkably rich in pure gold of a deep

AKINGHI, a large and populous town of Sudan, colour. in N lat. 10° 40', E long. 7° 34'; near the r. bank of the Mayanow river, which flows into the Niger below Dallu. Beyond this town, to the NE, Clapperton's route lay for two days over a hilly country, thickly wooded, but little cultivated, till he approached Guari.

AKIR, a large v. of Syria, 4 hours NW from Bethshemesh, and 2 hours SSW from Ramleh. The name corresponds in Arabic to the Hebrew Ekron, with which Dr. Robinson is inclined to identify it. But there are no remains of antiquity here: "per- H haps," says Dr. R., "because the ancient houses, like the modern hovels, were built not of stone but of earth."
AKJAHTA'SH, a v. of Asia Minor, 3 hours to the N of the road between Kalajik and Angora. Mr. Hamilton found numerous remains of antiquity here.
AKKA, a caravan-station on the route from Marocco to Timbuctu, on the margin of the Sahara, in N lat. 28° 32′, W long. 8° 9′.
AKKA. See ACRE.

AKKAR (JEBEL), the northern termination of the chain of Mount Libanus in Central Syria, under the parallel of 34° 20'. The country lying between the eastern flanks of the Jebel Akkar and the sea on the W, and between the Nahr-el-Bered, 3 hours N of Tripoli, and the Nahr-el-Kebir, 2 hours further N, is known as the district of A., and produces excellent fruit. The v. of the same name stands on the mouth of the Wadi Akkar, 18 m. NNE of Tripoli.
AKKAS, a small town of Russian Finland, in the district and 30 m. NW of Tavastehus.
AKKASOKI, a mountain of Norwegian Finland to the SW of Talvig. It is nearly isolated, and has an alt. of 3,395 ft. above the Altenfiord.
AKKEHU, a v. of Bokhara, about half-way between Balkh and Andhkoo, in N lat. 36° 50'.

AKKERKUF, or AGERKOUF, called also TEL NIMROD, and by the Turks NIMBOD TEPESSI, [i.e. 'the Mound of Nimrod,'] an enormous pile of ruins, 9 m. WNW from Bagdad. It stands upon a hillock sloping gently upwards to a height of about 125 ft. above the level of the surrounding plain, according to Rich; but Mignan assigns this elevation to the brickwork alone, and adds other 20 ft. for the height of the rubbish which now forms its pedestal. It is to be remarked also that the ground begins to rise in a gentle ascent for a furlong around the tower, occasioned, Ives imagines, by the rubbish of the old buildings which once stood here. The mass of the pile, which is solid, is composed of unburnt bricks mixed with chopped straw, having layers of reeds two inches thick between every seventh course. The bricks are 12 inches square, and 42 in. thick. These reeds, protruding from the weather-worn edge of the bricks, communicate to the profile of the edifice a singular serrated look. As in the Birs Nimrod, there are also here square holes running through the body of the pile, as if to afford ventilation, or perhaps to support the scaffolding employed in its erection. Mignan says: "Large wooden beams are passed through, apparently to strengthen the huge fabric." [Travels in Chaldea, p. 105.] It is now impossible to trace with accuracy the original outline of the building; but it seems to have been a square. The circuit, taken above the mound of rubbish,-which, however, is much above the real artificial base-is 300 ft., or according to Mignan 400 ft., and it measures across at the broadest part 100 ft. Mignan says that in the NE face, nearly in the centre, is an aperture resembling a Gothic window. The mound itself consists of loose sandy earth, probably drifted in part by the wind, mingled with fragments of brick, pottery, and half-vitrified clay. Its vicinity to Bagdad accounts sufficiently for the disappearance of its baked bricks and other transportable materials. The opinion of the natives generally is that this structure was originally designed as a beacon-post, on which signal-fires might be kindled. Niebuhr suggests that some ancient sovereign or caliph may have raised this vast mound as the site of a summer-house, in which fresher and cooler air might at all times be enjoyed than on the plain below. Mr. Buckingham sees in it the debris of a pyramid. M. Coupperie, in the Annales de la Propagation de la Foi, for 1830, advocates the theory, espoused also by Ives, that it may have been the site of a grand observatory. The accompanying sketch is from Mignan, who visited this structure in 1827. Another sketch is given in Sir R. K. Porter's Travels, vol. ii.- Rich's Memoir on the Ruins of Babylon.-Niebuhr's Travels in Arabia.—Mignan's Travels in Chaldaa. AKR
AKKIBOSA, a large town of Youriba in Africa, between Aggidiba and Adja. It is surrounded inside the walls with an impenetrable wood.—Clapperton.

AKKONADA (Ras), a cape on the Barbary coast, 7 m. W of Algiers, 3½ m. NE by E 3/4 E from Sidi Halif, and 3\frac{1}{2} m. W \frac{1}{2} S from Point Pessada. Beyond this cape—which is also known as CAPE CAXINES—the coast trends to the SW, and forms a great bay, of which the western side is defined by the peninsula of Sidi Ferruch.
AKKRAFIALL, a mountain on the SW coast of Iceland, in the neighbourhood of Reikiavik, having an alt. of about 1,500 ft.—Henderson.
AKKRUM, a v. of Holland, in the centre of the prov. of Friesland, on the r. bank of the Kroom, from which a canal runs off here to the Snecker-meer. Pop. 750.
AKKULLA, a town of Armenia, on the shore of the lake of Gokcheh or Sevan, which is here 16 m. broad.

AKLAN, a river of Siberia, which rises 30 m. NW of the town of Aklansk, and flows SE to the Penjina, which it joins a little above its outlet at the head of the gulf of Penjina, after a course of about 60 m.
AKLANSK, a town of Siberia, in the gov. of Irkutzk, on the l. bank of the above river, 50 m. WNW of Penjinsk.
AKLAT. See AKHLAT.
AKLIMAN. See AGALIMAN.

AKLO'M, a river of Bulgaria, in the sanjak of Nigebóle, which rises to the SE of Rasgrad, passes that town, and flows in a WNW course to join the Kara Lom at a point 16 m. S of Rustchuk on the r. bank of the Danube, where the united streams flow into that river under the name of the Lom.
AKMAGALI, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, on the r. bank of the Muishtah, on the road from Teheran to Koomabad.
AKMETCHET. See ACMETCHET.

AKNUR, a decayed town of India, in the Northern Punjab, at the base of the lowest range of the Himalaya, 20 m. N of Jamu; in N lat. 32° 42', E long. 74° 41'. It stands near the r. or W bank of the Chenab, which is navigable from a short distance above A. to the sea, and which, according to Vignè, has here a breadth of 100 yards; but Broome and Cunningham found that river flowing here, in the month of August when swollen by the rains, in seven channels, of which the broadest was 920 yards wide, while others were breast deep, and all had rapid currents. Vigne says the view of A. from the E bank of the river is very picturesque, but presents quite the appearance of a ruined city. The approach to it from the S lay through jungle; on the next day's march to the N, he found the surface of the country seemed "as if the tempestuous waves of a short sea had been suddenly changed into hills of sandstone and alluvium."
AKO, a town of Japan, on the S coast of Nifon, 100 m. S of Miaco.—See also Akho.
AKOBE, a town of Ladakh, on the r. bank of the Para river, in about N lat. 32° 32'.
AKOGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 32 m. W of Bhurtpur.
AKOJOGOLE, a town of Sinde, on the l. bank of the Indus, 45 m. N by W of Hydrabad.
AKOLAH, a city of India, in the Nizam territory, 120 m. NE by E of Aurungabad, 48 m. SW of Ellichpur. It is surrounded by extensive ruins.
AKOUGLAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, 105 m. ENE of Nakchivan, in a rich and fertile district.
AKRA. See ACCRA.

AKRABI, a district of about 20 sq. m. on the S coast of Arabia, inhabited by the tribe of Akrabi Arabs, whose chief v., Bizar Ahmed, 61 m. N of the promontory known as the Ass's Ears, contains about 250 inhabitants. It is bounded on the NE by the territory of the Abdali and the Howsheibi Arabs, and on the W by the Subaihi territory. The chief produce of the soil is jowari or millet, of which a considerable quantity is exported; they also export coffee and cotton. In the interior are thick forests of acacia.

AKROBAT, a pass leading from Cabul into Turkistan, half-way between Bamian and Syghan or Sykhan, a distance of 30 m. "I had expected," says Burnes, on ascending this pass, "to find the great snowy mountains beyond us; but we now looked upon them in a range behind." Its elevation is 10,200 ft. above sea-level, or 1,804 ft. above Bamian. The defile is 5 m. in length through a limestone and slate formation. It forms, says a recent visitor, [Asiatic Journ., June 1844,] a bellows for all the winds of the snowy range to concentrate in; and has consequently both in summer and winter a very low temperature. Tiflis. At the end of the month of June, this writer found it "intensely cold." There is little cultivation in the valley into which this pass leads on the N side. It is "barren and desolate, without participating in the wild and grand features which generally characterize these regions."—Burnes.—Wood.—Griffiths.
AKROFROOM, a town of Guinea, 80 m. N by W of Cape Coast Castle, on a head-branch of the Bu-

AKRON, a town of New York, U. S., in Erie co., sumpra river. 268 m. W of Albany, on Muddy creek. Pop. 300 .- Also a town of Ohio, U. S., the capital of Summit co., at the junction of the Ohio and Erie, and the Ohio and Pennsylvanian canals, 38 m. S of Cleveland. It is a place of active trade. Pop. 1,665. By a succession of locks, the Ohio and Erie canal rises suddenly at A. to the Portage summit.

AKSAI, or Jaksai, a river in the Russian gov. of Georgia, rising in the NE flanks of Mount Tersh, and flowing NNE to the Khargina branch of the Terek river. It at one time formed the boundary line in this quarter betwixt Russia and Persia; but is now removed by half the length of Georgia from the Per-
AKSAKA'L, a large lake, or chain of lakes prosian frontiers. bably, in Tartary, in the great Kirghiz steppe, about 60 m. NE of Lake Aral, formed by the waters of the Turghai and the Irghiz rivers.

AKSEENSKAIA, a town of Russia, in the territory of the Don Cossacks, 90 m. SW by S from Sarepta, on a stream flowing S to the Don. AKSERAT, [i. e. 'White palace,'] a town of Asiatic Turkey, the capital of a sanjak of the same name, 16 hours N by E of Karabunar, 94 m. SSE of Angora; in N lat. 38° 20' [Hamilton]; situated in an open and well cultivated valley, through which the Beyáysú flows into the salt lake of Kóch-hisar. It contains 800 Mahommedan and a few Armenian houses. It is perhaps the ancient Archelais or Arche- Lais Colonia of the emperor Claudius. AKSHEHR, [i. e. 'White town,'] a town of Asiatie Turkey, in the pashalik of Karamania and sanjak of Akshehr, 7 hours NNE from Arkutkhan, and 11 hours SW of Bulavadin, at the foot of the Sultan- Tagh, which has an elevation of from 1,000 to 1,500 ft. above the level of the plain of A., while the elevation of the plain itself may be 3,000 ft. [Ainsworth.] It is said to contain 1,500 houses, which rise above one another in terraces, or are scattered around groves and gardens which contain a great variety of fruit-trees; but the streets are narrow, and present numerous ruined mosques and houses. Its principal ornament is a handsome mosque and college. About 6 m. to the N of this town, is a lake of the same name surrounded by a marshy border, and of varying dimensions according to the season. When Kinneir AKT visited this district in the month of February, he found the lake extending nearly the whole way from Akshehr to Ketchluk, or about 17 m. NW by W, and filled with reeds. And on turning to the N to enter Bulavadin or Baloudeen—as he writes it—the plain was one entire sheet of water from 2 to 3 ft. deep; but Mr. W. G. Browne, who passed through the same district in the month of August, does not notice this feature of the country [Walpole's Travels, vol. iii., p. 118]; confirming Colonel Leake's remark, that these lakes "receive the torrents running from the surrounding mountains, and are greatly enlarged in winter, but in summer are entirely dried up." [Ibid. p. 221.] A. is thought by D'Anville and Kinneir to be the ancient Thymbrium. Colonel Leake supposes it to be Julia or Juliopolis; while Mr. W. J. Hamilton [Geog. Journ., vol. viii., p. 144] identifies it with the ancient Philomelium.
AKSIBUK, a town of Georgia, 60 m. SSW of

AKSU, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Anatolia, and sanjak of Khudávendikár, 15 m. SE of Brussa. At the 8th m. from A. a magnificent view is obtained of the city of Brussa and its verdant plain, contrasted with the cliffs and snowy summits of Olympus. [Kinneir.]—There is a river of this name in Kurdistan, which rises in the district of Lahijan, and traverses the district of Seradusht to the Tigris .- Also a river in Asiatic Turkey which flows into the Black sea between Akchah Shehr and Eregli.

AKSU, a district of Little Bukharia, bounded on the N by the mountains of Allak; on the E by the district of Keutch; on the S by Kholen and Yarkand; and on the W by Yunpin. It is watered by several streams which are said to lose themselves in the steppes; and is well-cultivated, producing lentils, wheat, barley, millet, and cotton; besides apricots, grapes, melons, and other fruits in abundance. Its capital, of the same name, is situated in N lat. 41° 9', 150 m. NE of Yarkand. Timkowski says it contains 6,000 houses, that it is the residence of an ambass appointed by the Chinese government; and that it is frequented for purposes of trade by Chinese, Kirghise, the natives of Southern and Eastern Turkistan, Kashmerians, and the inhabitants of India and Tashkend. The inhabitants manufacture much cotton stuff, and their embroidered saddles and bridles of deer-skin are highly prized throughout Turkistan. On the road between Guldja and A. there are some manufactures of copper and iron which are wrought by criminals exiled from the interior provinces of China; there is also a ruby mine in the neighbourhood of A. Moorcroft was informed that this district contains about 30,000 houses; and that the Aksu river, which traverses it, rises in several streams which unite to the N of Turfan; then flows 3 days' journey S to Aksu, and, still holding a southern direction for 5 days' journey further, falls into a river which flows eastwards to Saceran, and onwards to Kamul, a large city situated in China.—Geog. Journ., vol. i.—Timkowski's Mission.
AKSU'M. See AXUM.
AKSURRAI, a river of Northern India, formed by the union of the Ghorri, Bungi, and Furkhar, at a point 13 m. NW of Kunduz. It reaches the Oxus by a northerly course of about 40 m.—Elphinstone.

AKTAGH, a mountain range of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Rúm or Sivas, between the parallels of 39° and 40° N, running SW and NE between the head-streams of the Kizil Irmak, through a district of which little is known by modern geographers.—Also a mountain of Anatolia, near the sources of the Rhyndacus and the Hermus. Alt. 8,000 ft.—A range bearing the same name, and nearly under the meridian of 42° E. connects the Scherian Dagh with the UNIVERS Tshekma Dagh, in the great plateau dividing the upper waters of the Araxes from those of the Murad.

AKTAVAR, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, on the r. bank of the Hermus, near its source, 6 m. N of Ghiédiz, on the NE flank of the Aktagh, containing about 400 houses. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in cotton-weaving.
AKTCHIM, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Perm, on the r. bank of the Vishera, 52 m. ENE of Tsherdin.

AKTEBOLI, or AGHTEBO'LI', or GATAPOLI, a small port of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Kirkkilisa, in N lat. 42° 4', E long. 27° 59', 7 m. N by W from Cape Reyveh, and 69 m. from the entrance of the Bosphorus.
AKTENI GOLI, a lake of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Boli, in the SE corner of the picturesque plain of Dúzchah, through which the Milán river flows .- Ainsworth.
AK TEPE, the extremity of a range of hills, in the SW of Khiva, on the shore of the Caspian, under the parallel of 38° 20' N.
AKTIAR. See SEBASTOPOL.
AKUL (DEIR EL), a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the r. bank of the Tigris, 44 m. below Bagdad.
AKUSHAS, a Lesghian tribe of Daghestan, forming a republic of about 30 villages on the banks of the Koisu. They are divided into twelve tribes, each of which has a chief.
AKUTAN. See ACOOTAN.
AKWAMBU, or AGUAMBO, a state of Guinea, on the Rio Volta, to the N of Aquapim.
AKYAB, a district of Aracan comprehending the town of Aracan, and taking its name from a harbour on the coast, in N lat. 20° 8', E long. 92° 54'. The and is the residence of a British commissioner.
AKYAZI, the name of several kadiliks, or jurisdictions, in Natolia.
AKZIB, or ACHZIB, a town of Syria, on the coast, 9 m. N of Acre; supposed to be the Acdippa or Ecdippon of Pliny and Josephus. It is situated on a rising ground, near the bed of a winter-torrent.

ALA, a town of Tyrol, on the l. bank of the Adige, 10 m. S of Roveredo. Pop. 3,024.—Also a town of Sardinia, in the district of Sassari, on the Selema river.—Also a town of Japan, in the island of Ximo, 18 m. NE of Naka.
ALA ORILLO DEL RIO, a town of Texas, on the l. bank of the Rio Grande, in N lat. 34° 27'.

ALABAMA, one of the SW states of North America; bounded on the N by Tennessee; on the E by Georgia; on the S by Florida, and the gulf of Mexico; and on the W by the Mississippi. It lies between 30° 12′ and 35° N lat.; and 8° and 11° 30′ W long. from Washington. It is 317 m. in length from N to S; and 174 m. from E to W; and contains 46,000 sq. m., according to Davenport; 52,000 according to Goodrich. It has about 60 m. of sea-coast. Nearly in the middle of the coast-line is the spacious bay of Mobile which extends 30 m. inland. Massacre and Dauphin islands belong to this state. The coast is low and level, little elevated above the level of the gulf, and covered with pine, cyprus, and loblolly. In the central districts, the surface is hilly and undulating, with a natural timber-growth of black and white oak, hickory, poplar, cedar, chestnut, pine, and mulberry. The Alleghany mountains, or western extremity of the Appalachian chain, terminate in the NE of this state where they sink to hills of moderate elevation. More than half the surface of the state is what is called a pine barren, consisting of lands of a gray or reddish clayey soil, covered with coarse grass, and a thin sprinkling of trees. In the northern parts, ice is frequently found in winter, but running waters ALA are rarely frozen, and cattle require no shelter. The heat of summer is very oppressive, though somewhat tempered by breezes from the gulf of Mexico. During the hottest season, none but Negroes, and those acclimated, can remain with safety upon the low banks of the rivers, or on the swampy lands. Cotton is the staple production. On the gulf, the sugar-cane is sometimes grown. Indian-corn, rice, wheat, and oats are raised.—Geologically considered, the southern level district has features similar to the portion further N, consisting of beds of sand and clay, and calcareous strata referrible to the latest tertiary and newer secondary periods. The N region consists chiefly of sandstone and limestone of the carboniferous group. The rocky ledge which marks the division between it and the plain is sandstone. Coal, salt, and iron abound in this section; gold occurs in some of the NE counties.—The Mobile is the principal river of The Alabama branch, formed by the Tallapoosa A. and Coosa, is navigable for vessels drawing 6 ft. of water to Claiborne, 60 m. above its junction; and has from 4 to 5 ft. for 150 m. higher up. The Tombigbee is navigable for steam-boats to Columbus in Mississippi, 306 m. from its mouth. The Black Warrior or Tuscoloosa, a branch of the Tombigbee, is navigable for steam-boats to 30 m. above its junction. The Chattahoochee, which through half its course is common to A. and Georgia, is navigable to Columbia in Georgia 300 m. The Tennessee has a course of 200 m. from NE to SW, and WNW, within the northern part of this state. The pop. of A. in 1810 was under 10,000. Its increase has been as follows:- Of the population of 1840, 253,532 were slaves; and the ratio of the aggregate town-population to the whole population of the state was 2.8 per cent. The proportion of blind was 1 in 2,827; of deaf and dumb. 1 in 2,614; of idiots, 1 in 1,655. The cotton crop of 1837 amounted to 330,000 bales, or 130,000,000 lbs., valued at nearly 15,000,000 dollars. In 1842, the crop was 318,315 bales, of which 185,414 bales were exported to Great Britain. The crop of 1848 was 165,000,000 lbs. In 1840 there were 14 cotton manufactories, with 1,502 spindles, in the state; and the quantity of cotton raised amounted to 117,138,823 lbs. The agricultural produce in the same year was 20,947,004 bushels of Indian corn; 1,406,353 h. of oats; 828,052 b. of wheat; 149,019 lbs. of rice; 273,353 lbs. of tobacco; 10,143 lbs. of sugar. The total value of the agricultural products of 1848 was estimated at 27,300,000 dollars; of taxable property at 276,000,000 dollars.—The total value of exports in the year ending June 30, 1840, was 12,854,694 dollars; of imports, 574,651 dollars. In 1845, the exports were 10,538,228 dollars; imports, 473,491 dollars. The state is divided into 49 counties:- The Southern division includes the counties of Mobile, Baldwin, Washington, Clarke, Sumter, Marengo, Monroe, Conecuh, Covington, Butler, Dale, Coffee, Henry, Barbour, Pike, Montgomery, Lowndes, and Wilcox. The Middle division includes the counties of Russell, Chambers, Randolph, Macon, Tallapoosa, Coosa, Autauga, Dallas, Perry, Grene, Tuscaloosa, Pickens, Fayette, Marion, Walker, Jefferson, Bibb, and Shelby. The Northern division includes the counties of Lauderdale, Franklin, Lawrence, Limestone, Madison, Morgan, Blount, St. Clair, Marshall, Jackson, De Kalb, Cherokee, Benton, and Talladega. There is a canal, 36 m. in length, with 6 ft. water, and 60 ft. breadth at surface, from the head of the Muscle shoals in the Tennessee, along the r. bank of that river, to Florence. The Huntsville canal, from Triena on the Tennessee, down the Indian creek to Huntsville, is 16 m. in length.-The Alabama and Florida railroad extends from Pensacola to Montgomery 156 m.; with a branch of 10 m. from Selma to Cahawba. The Montgomery and Westpoint railroad, 87 m. long, extends from the northern termination of the A. and tensive. F. railroad at Montgomery, to Westpoint at the head of the rapids of Chattahoochee, 30 m. above Columbus. The Tescumbia and Decatur railroad, betwixt these places, is 44 m. in length. The Wetumpka, connecting the Tennessee and Alabama rivers at Wetumpka, is 10 m. in length.—Tuscaloosa, on the Black Warrior river, was the seat of government, but has been recently changed to Montgomery, a town a little SE of the centre of the state, named after General Montgomery who fell at the storming of Quebec. Mobile, on the W side of Mobile bay, is the chief seat of commerce. The other principal towns are Cahawba, Huntsville, Florence, and Wetumpka.-The Baptists and the Methodists are prevailing religious denominations. In 1840, there were 114 academies and grammar-schools, with 5,018 students, and 639 primary schools with 16,243 scholars, within the state; but 22,592 free white persons, above 20 years of age, could neither read nor write. The university of A. at Tuscaloosa was founded in 1820. In 1846 it had 8 professors, and was attended by 80 students. The governor of A. is elected biennially by the people. He must be a native citizen of the U.S. He has a salary of 2,500 dollars. The senate consists of 33 members, elected for 4 years, one-half going out every second year. The house of representatives consists of 100 members, elected for two years. The pay of members of both houses is 4 dollars a-day. The judges of all the courts are elected, for six years, by a joint vote of the two houses of assembly.—The finances of the state in November 1845 were as follows:- Dollars. Balance 27th Nov., 1845, . . . Principal Items of Expenditure. Pay of members and other expenses of the legisla- 48,039 48 36,165 41
159,645 83 ture, session 1844.'45, Salaries of state officers, . Interest on state bonds, 12,102 34 Conveying convicts to the penitentiary, All other expenses, Prosecution of criminals, 2,965 13 28,133 30 Total, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Chief Sources of Income. Bonus from the bank of Mobile, . . . 5,000 00 Taxes of 1843, 265,955 59 From a report of a committee of the legislature in January, Taxes of 1844, . 1846, it appears that the debt of the state was Dollars. Total, . . . . The state bonds fall due as follows: 1,113,000 dollars at 6 per cent. in 1850 300,000 dollars at 5 per cent. in 1852 1,043,555 dollars at 5 per cent. in 1858 600,000 dollars at 5 per cent. in 1859 3,843,000 dollars at 5 per cent. in 1863 959,000 dollars at 5 per cent. in 1865 2,317,000 dollars at 5 per cent, in 1866 184,707 80 The interest on state bonds payable in England, 286,800 00 18,000 00 do. do. on university-fund, 68,238 00 on common school-fund, do. do. 557,745 80 The interest has heretofore been punctually paid. But a state Paper of 1849 represents the state bonds as amounting to 9,385,936 dollars; and the annual liabilities to 648,576 dollars; while the revenue only amounts to 524,776 dollars. And it is hinted that ALA the payment of the bonds which fall due in 1850, and of which 1,013,000 dollars are held in London, will be postponed. We are hopeful, however, that the resources of this state are still ex- The southern portion of this state was originally a part of Florida; and the northern was included in Georgia. In 1802, Georgia ceded all her territory W of the Chattahoochee to the United States, and this portion was united with the Mississippi territory as a territorial government, in 1817. In 1820 it was admitted to the Union as an independent state.

ALABAMA, a river of North America, in the above state, formed by the junction of the Coosa and the Tallapoosa near Fort Jackson. From this point of confluence, the A. flows SSW to its junction with the Tombigbee coming from the NW. The united streams then assume the name of the Mobile river, and under that name pursue a course of 48 m. to Mobile bay. The A. is navigable from above its junction with the Tombigbee, by vessels drawing 6 ft., 60 m. to Claiborne; from Claiborne to the mouth of the Cahawba, 150 m., it has from 4 to 5 ft. water; and from the Cahawba to its head-branches it has about 3 ft.; but it is subject to great alternations of depth according to the season and the rains.-Also a town of New York, U. S., in Genesee co., 12 m. NW of Batavia, on the Tonawanda creek, which has a fall of 30 ft. in the neighbourhood. Pop. 1,798.
ALABAT, one of the Philippine group in the Chinese sea, in N lat. 14° 10′, E long. 122°. It lies across the mouth of Lamon bay, off the E coast of the isle of Lucon.-Horsburgh's chart, No. ii.
ALA'BLE, a port and fishing v. of Asiatic Turkey, on the S coast of the Black sea, at the mouth of the Elæus. It has been identified with the ancient Elæum, but presents no remains of antiquity.
ALABOUK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Tamboo, on the Verona river, 78 m. S by E of Tamboo.
ALACH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, W from Erfurth. Pop. 540.

ALACHUA, a county of East Florida, U. S. It embraces a large extent of good land, and has a superficial area of 2,500 sq. m. Pop. 2,282, of whom, in 1841, 562 were slaves. It is intersected by the Wakassassee river, and skirted on the W by the Suwanee. Its surface is rolling, but towards the coast flat and wet. There is a marshy plain in this co., nearly 15 m. in breadth, and 50 in circumference, known as A. savannah, which is supposed to discharge its waters by an underground passage into Lake

ALACRANES, a shoal off the coast of Yucatan, Orange. in the gulf of Mexico, extending 14 m. from N to S, and 11 m. from E to W. There are three islets in the southern part of the shoal, namely, Perez, Chica, and Pajaros; and a harbour is formed in the reefs which extend from Perez, in N lat. 22° 23', W long. 89° 42'. The proximity of this shoal is generally avoided by navigators.
ALACTA, a river of Guatimala, running S, and nearly on the 86th meridian, into the N side of the lake of Nicaragua.
ALADA. See ARDRA.

ALA'DAGH, a mountain range of Asiatic Turkey, between Lake Van on the S, and the Akbulak Dagh on the N. The head-stream of the Morad sweeps round the S and W flanks of this range.-Also a range in Anatolia, to the NW of Angora, extending between the Ischik Dagh on the NE, and the valley of the Sangarius on the S and W.
ALADAGH SU, a river of Anatolia, descending from the SW flank of the Ala Dagh to the Ayala or Sangarius. It is probably the ancient Siberis.
ALADCHALA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Romelia, on the road from Adrianople to Kezanlik.
ALADINE ISLANDS, a name given by some navigators to a group in the Mergui archipelago, lying to the SW of St. Matthew's, between 98° and 98° 10' E long. The largest of the group is Davis' island.
ALADJALAR, a town of Romelia, on the l. bank of the Paschaka, 20 m. SW of Philippopolis.
ALADJAM, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Sivas, near the mouth of the Aladjam Chai, which flows into the bay of Sinope, 16 m. WNW of Bafra.
ALAFOENS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 6 m. NE of Viseu.
ALAFVA, a small town of Ostero Bothnia, in Sweden, 121 m. N of Ijo. Glass is manufactured here.
ALAGADO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, flowing into the Araguaya, on the l. bank, in S lat. 12° 45'.
ALAGOA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarva, 7 m. S of Silves. Pop. 1,000.—Also a town of St. Michael's, one of the Azores.

ALAGOAS, a province of Brazil, on the l. bank of the San Francisco which separates it from Sergipe. Its pop. amounts to about 130,000. It exports sugar, cotton, hides, Brazil-wood, and rose-wood; its tobacco also is of excellent quality, but sugar is the staple article of cultivation. It derives its name from its old cap., which is in S lat. 9° 39½, 103 m. S by W of Olinda Recife, near the mouth of the San Miguel das Alagoas, a small river which flows from two lakes to the NW. Its principal port is Maceió.

ALAGON, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. WNW of Saragossa, on a peninsula formed by the confluence of the Ebro and Xilosa or Jalon, and on the r. bank of the latter. Numerous remains of Roman grandeur exist in the vicinity of A. Its citadel is the work of the Moors. The Canal Imperial de Aragon crosses the Jalon on the S side of A.—Also a large river in Estremadura, which, descending from the Sierra d'Estrella, flows through the plains of Plasencia, passing Coria on the r. bank, and joins the Tagus, above Alcantara, after a course of 70 m.

ALAGUASH, a river in N. America, which carries the waters of lakes Umgenayagamnic, Pagaquaymuc, and Wulagasquigwam, by a NE by N course of about 70 m., and a fall of 348 ft., into the St. John's river, at a point nearly on the 69th meridian. Its course lies wholly within the territory assigned to the United States by the treaty of Washington, August 9, 1842. It is full of falls and rapids; and its cataracts present an insurmountable obstacle to navigation.
ALAH DAGH. See ALA DAGH.

ALAH-SHAHIR, or ALA SHEHR, [i. e. 'the Beautiful city,'] a considerable town of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Aidin, 86 m. E of Smyrna; 16 m. NNW of Bulavadin, on the plain of the Hermus or Cogamus, midway between that river and the base of Mount Tmolus. It contains about 300 houses occupied by Greeks, and nearly 3,000 inhabited by Turks. The modern buildings are of mean aspect, and the streets narrow; but the town, spreading over the slopes of three or four hills, surrounded by cultivated gardens and vineyards, and seated in one of the richest and most extensive plains in Asia Minor, presents an imposing appearance when viewed at a distance, and commands a magnificent prospect. Elliott approaching it from Sardis, or from WNW, says: "From the caféné of Saliklee to A. the distance is for many miles gradually approach each other, and as the valley becomes narrower, the heights of Tmolus lose something of their elevation, preparing to merge themselves in the plain. A little beyond the town, they are seen no more; and the opposite chain, crossing to the SE, meets that of Taurus." A. is the seat of a Greek archbishop; and occupies the site of the ancient Philadelphia, whose mutilated remains, in the ALA form of fragments of rich cornices, truncated shafts of columns, and sarcophagi, meet the observant eye at every step. It derived its early name from its founder, Attalus Philadelphus, and was the second city in Lydia. Tacitus mentions it among the towns restored by Tiberius after an earthquake which partially or wholly destroyed fourteen cities. Not long before the date of the Apocalypse, P. had suffered so much from earthquakes, that it had been in a great measure deserted by its inhabitants. It appears, says Gibbon, to have resisted the attacks of the Turks in 1312 with more success than other cities. "At a distance from the sea, -- forgotten by the emperor,-encompassed on all sides by the Turks,-her valiant citizens defended their religion and freedom above fourscore years, and at length capitulated with the proudest of the Ottomans (Bajazet) in 1390. Among the Greek colonies and churches of Asia, P. is still erect—a column in a scene of ruins."—Cramer's Asia Minor.—Rev. F. J. Arundell's visit to the Seven Churches of Asia.—Elliott's Travels.

ALAHUELA, or ALAJUELA, a large town of Central America, in the state of Costa Rica, midway between Cartago and the W coast. It is a place of considerable trade; and is connected with Puente d'Arena, a commodious port in the gulf of Nicoya, by a good mule road.
ALAIGNE, a town and cant. of France, in the dep. of l'Aude, arrond. of Limoux. Pop. of cant., comprising 27 communes, in 1831, 8,099; of town in 1842, 524.
ALAINCOURT, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Aisne, and arrond. of Saint-Quentin, on the Avre. Pop. 754.
ALAIRAC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Aude, and arrond. of Carcassone. Pop. 580.

ALAIS, or ALETS, a cant., com., town, and arrond. of France, in the dep. of Gard, at the foot of the Cevennes.—The arrond. has an area of 135,576 hectares. Its pop. in 1836 was 83,091. It comprises the cant. of Alais, Saint-Ambroix, Anduze, Barjac, Genolhac, Saint-Jean-du-Gard, Ledignan, Saint-Martin-de- Valgalgues, and Vezenobres.—The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 16,647 in 1831.—The town is situated on the l. bank of the Gardon, 25 m. NW of Nîmes; in N lat. 47° 7'. It is a place of great antiquity, being the Alesia of Cæsar's Commentaries. The pop. in 1789 was 9,620; in 1842, 15,834. The majority are Protestants. There are a communal college in the town, a tribunal of commerce, and a mining school. It also possesses a public library of 4,000 vols. Its chief trade consists in the manufacture and export of ribands, and in the produce of its iron-furnaces and coal-mines. Extensive coal-mines have been wrought here since 1809; iron, lead, zinc, and manganese are also wrought; and there are mineral springs of some repute in the environs. A railroad 273 m. in length connects A. with Nîmes.—A. is the birth-place of the celebrated chemist Dumas. It long adhered to the Huguenot cause; but the humiliation of that party was completed by the treaty of June 27, 1643, which was concluded at Alais.
ALAJAH-HISAR, or KRUSHEVATZ, a v. and kadilik of Romelia, on the Western Morava, formerly the residence of the beg.

ALAKANANDA, or ALAKNUNDA, a river of 21 hours. The two ranges of hills which run parallel | Northern Hindostan, in Gurwal, one of the headstreams of the Ganges. It rises in the Himalaya range, near the Manah Ghaut pass, under the 31st parallel, and flowing SSW is joined on the r. by the Douli descending from Niti Ghaut, near Joseemuth, at 6,300 ft. above sea-level. From Joseemuth the general course of the A. is WSW to Kurnprag, where it receives the Pindar on the l. It then flows E to Roodurprag, where it receives a large stream on the r. called the Caligunga, which rises at Kedarnauth and has a course nearly SSW. Its course is WSW from this latter point to Devaprayaga or Deoprang, in N lat. 30° 9', E long. 78° 33', where it is joined by the western head-branch of the Ganges, the Bhaghiretti. At the point of confluence, the A. is the larger river, having a breadth of 142 ft., and rising after heavy rains 47 ft. above its ordinary level. It contains several species of large fish.
ALAK NOR, a lake of Mongolia, in N lat. 40° 20', near the route from Khamil SW to Soutchou in the NW corner of China.
ALA' KOI, a v. of 100 Armenian and 30 Kurd families, near the E side of Lake Van, 20 m. SW of Merek.
ALAK OMO, or Chara Omo, a lake of Mongolia, under the parallel of 39° 30' N.
ALAKTUKUL, a large lake of Chinese Tartary, under the parallel of 45° 30' N, 130 m. N of Guldja Kureh. It is said to stretch 75 m. from NW to SE, and to have a breadth of 45 m.
ALA KYUN, a town of Burmah, on the r. bank of the Irawaddi, 68 m. WSW of Umerapura.
ALALA, a town of Russian Georgia, on the l. bank of the Alazan, between Shignak and Sheki.
ALALAS, a v. of La Plata, in the Llanos de Manso, near the l. bank of the Vermejo.

ALAMA, a v. of Spain, in Aragon, on the Xalon river, 12 m. WSW of Calatayud. It was at one time strongly fortified, and was held by the Moors till 1122. -Also a river of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, rising near Villarraso, 18 m. ENE of Soria, and flowing from SW to NE to the Ebro, which it joins, after a course of 30 m., near Alfaro.
ALAMBRA. See ALHAMBRA.
ALAMEDA, a town of Spain, in Salamanca, 12 m. NNW of Ciudad Rodrigo, 9 m. E of Almeida.— Also a v. 6 m ENE of Madrid.
ALAMILLA, a town of Mexico, on the l. bank of the Rio Bravo del Norte, 60 m. S of Santa Fe.
ALAMINOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. ENE of Guadalaxara.

ALAMO, a town of Spain, 21 m. SW of Madrid. -Also a presidio in Texas near the town of San Antonio.—Also a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, 50 m. WSW of Culiacan.—Also a v. in Kalamazoo, co. Michigan, U. S.
ALAMO MOCHA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Cohahuila, 50 m. N of Castanuela.

ALAMOS (Los), a rich mining-district of Mexico, in a barren plain between the rivers Mayo and El Fuerte, in the prov. of Sinaloa. The mines lie nearly 5 leagues N of the town of Alamos, which is 24 leagues N of El Fuerte. The country betwixt these towns is level and practicable for carriages. The pop. of the town of A. is about 7,000.—Ward.

ALAMUT, an elevated district of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, watered by the Sharud or southern branch of the Kizil Ouzan, and celebrated in history as having been the residence of the chief of the Assassins or 'Old Man of the Mountain.' It is separated on the N by a high range from Ghilan and Mazanderan; on the S is the Pishaku range. The ascent to this district from that of Mir in the plain to the S, is by the very steep pass of Duderán, leading in a NW direction, and the summit of which is about 8 m. from Mir. The rock of Alamut, known also by the name of 'the Vulture's nest,' on which the Sheikh al Jebal or chief of the Assassins, whose mandates were obeyed from the Oxus to the Mediterranean, had his fortress, is a bare ridge, 3 m. distant from the range of mountains which separates Alamut from Ghilan. The ridge has a length of 300 yards from E to W, and is very narrow on the top, not exceeding 20 or 25 ft. Its height on the N, S, and E sides is ALA about 200 ft.; on the W side 100 ft. The remains of a few bastions, and of a wall which has encircled the rock, may yet be traced. The rock is divided into two parts at the summit, one of which is higher than the other. The lower part of the mountain has been formed into terraces. "In the vicinity of the rock all is desolation and barrenness; not a single habitation disturbs the melancholy solitude of the scene where the eagle and the lizard are now the sole tenants." There are no ruins or mounds in the vicinity, and no inscriptions, to denote the site of the city which Von Hammer describes as having existed here. Hulaku, the brother of the great Mogul conqueror Mangu Khan, took this fortress by storm; but Timur also was obliged to undertake its siege.
ALAN, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Haute Garonne, arrond. of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. 1,115.

ALAN (CAPE), a promontory on the W coast of the gulf of Georgia; in N lat. 48° 2', W long. 122° 6'. ALAND ISLANDS—pronounced Oland—a small archipelago, named by the Finlanders Ahvenamnæ, at the entrance of the gulf of Bothnia, about 20 m. long and 30 broad. The group-which is divided into three oblong clusters—consists of 60 inhabited islands, apparently of granitic formation, surrounded by 200 desert and stony islets, and having a total superficies of about 470 sq. m. It is separated from the coast of Sweden by the Alandshaff, a channel 24 m. in breadth; and from Finland by the Wattuskiftet, which is 14 m. across at its greatest width. The pop., numbering, according to Clarke, in 1800, between 5,000 and 6,000, but according to recent writers from 12,000 to 15,000, are chiefly employed in fishing, and in rearing cattle. The Alanders are daring seamen and expert fishermen. They are of Swedish origin, and speak the Swedish language. Each householder is his own carpenter, shoemaker, tailor, fisherman, baker, and miller. Sheep-skins form their common dress, and bags of dried sealskins serve for their shoes. During winter, they are chiefly occupied in fishing, by dragging their nets under the ice, or in hunting for and killing seals by shooting them.—The surface of these islands is gently undulated, full of rocks, intersected by numerous bays, sounds, and inlets of the sea which often present the appearance of large lakes, and covered in part with thin low forests. There are no rivers, but numerous small lakes. The soil is stony, and the earth so light that the crops seldom come to maturity. The pine, the beech, the alder, the hazel, and the birch, are the only trees which flourish upon them. Besides grain, potatoes, flax, and kitchen-vegetables are grown; and pasturage is found for about 14,000 small cattle, and as many sheep. Horses and goats are also reared, and a considerable quantity of cheese is made. The exports are dry and pickled fish, butter, cheese, hides, skins, salt beef, and firewood.—The principal island—from which the group is named—is situated in N lat. 60° 15′, E long. 20°. Its length according to Coxe is 40 m., and its breadth from 12 to 16 m., but this is certainly an over estimate. The best charts give it a length from N to S of 18 m.; and a breadth from E to W of 14 m. Its area is about 250 sq. m.; and its pop. amounts to about one-half of the whole group. The coast presents several good harbours, among others that of Ytternäs, a safe and deep harbour on the W side. Rye, barley, and wheat are grown upon it: the latter to the extent of 2,500 barrels yearly. The most remarkable place in this island is Castelholm, situated on an insular rock of red granite at the extremity of a tongue of land, the chateau in which Eric XIV., son and successor of Gustavus Vasa, was imprisoned in 1571, and which was the residence of the governor of Aland until 1634. A very narrow passage separates it from Ekeroe, the most westerly of the group, where a convent was founded before the Reformation. Near Ekeroe, and surrounded by rocks, is a little isle named Signilskær, upon which there is a telegraph and a pilot-station. The other important isles are Lemland, Lumparland, Fæglæ, Kumlinge, Brændæ, Vordee, and Hamne.-The island of Aland was governed by kings of its own prior to its dependency upon Finland, when it came under the domination of Sweden, and had its particular governors. In 1634, it was added to the captainship of Bierneborg, and composed a district and provost's court, including eight pastorates or parishes. In 1809 the entire archipelago was ceded to Russia, and reunited to the government of Abo-Bierneborg, from which it had been separated; and the line of their coast forms the frontier of the Russian empire on this side. These islands afford safe and commodious harbours to the Russian fleet, which from thence can watch the vessels in the lake of Mœlar, and the Swedish coasting-trade along the W of the gulf of Bothnia. The best account we yet possess of these islands will be found in Dr. E. D. Clarke's Travels, vol. xi., accompanied by a chart of the whole group. - Commercial Tariff. - Coxe's Travels .- Clarke's Travels .- Laing's Sweden .- Dictionnaire Geographique.

ALAND, a river of Prussian Saxony, formed by the union of the Biese and the Uchte, and some minor rivulets, a few m. to the S of Seehausen. Below that town, it turns to the NNW, and after pursuing a course parallel to the Elbe for 14 m., falls into it, on the l., at Schnackenburg. The railway from Magdeburg, which joins the Berlin railway at Wittenberge, crosses this river.
ALANDE'O, a mountain of the Junna range, near Dhoriman, in the Great Indian desert, where some brazen horses' heads are annually worshipped with peculiar solemnity on the 3d day of the 4th month.
ALANDRA. See ALHANDRA.
ALANDROAL, a town and castle of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, 7 m. S by E of Villaviciosa.
ALANIS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. NNE of Seville. A silver mine in the neighbourhood of this town was wrought by the Romans.
ALANNO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra Prima, 12 m. S of Civita di Penne. Pop. 1,537.
ALAPAIEVSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Perm, on the l. bank of the Neiva, 90 m. NNE of Ekaterineburg, in N lat. 57° 48'.
ALAPAPAHA, a river of Georgia, U.S., a branch of the Suwanee, having a course of about 100 m.
ALARA, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Karamania, 11 m. W by N of Alaya, near the mouth of a small stream of the same name.

ALARCON, a town of Spain, in New Castile, on a rock nearly insulated by the Xucar, 40 m. SE of Cuença. It occupies a strong military position; but is at present a small place of 500 inhabitants, with several handsome churches.
ALARGATE REEF, a shoal in the Caribbean sea, off the NE Mosquito coast; in N lat. 15° 6', W long. 82° 25'.
